text.title: tmux-power-user Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for tmux-power-user. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use Ctrl+A as the prefix key rather than the default Ctrl+B; it is more ergonomic and consistent with GNU screen muscle memory.
rule.rationale: Ctrl+B requires moving the left hand off the home row. Ctrl+A is reachable without moving the hand, reducing friction across hundreds of daily prefix-chord invocations.

rule.statement: Set base-index 1 and pane-base-index 1; the default zero-indexing makes Alt+1 select window 0, which feels counter-intuitive.
rule.rationale: With base-index 1, Alt+1 through Alt+9 map directly to the visual position of windows in the status bar.

rule.statement: Always set escape-time to 10ms or less; the default 500ms causes a noticeable delay in vim when pressing Escape.
rule.rationale: Vim and other editors rely on fast Escape detection. The default 500ms makes modal editing feel sluggish over SSH.

rule.statement: Use | and - for splits; the defaults " and % are not mnemonic. Open splits in the current pane's working directory.
rule.rationale: | visually represents a vertical divider (horizontal split). - represents a horizontal line (vertical split). Both are immediately memorable. Current-path inheritance eliminates the need to cd after each split.

rule.statement: Run the status bar script with a 5-second refresh interval; 1-second intervals cause noticeable CPU overhead on low-power VPS instances.
rule.rationale: A status bar that refreshes every second calls shell scripts 60 times per minute. At 5 seconds, the overhead drops to 12 calls per minute with no perceptible staleness for CPU/memory metrics.

rule.statement: Install TPM before adding any plugin declarations to .tmux.conf; otherwise tmux.conf fails to source and all settings are lost.
rule.rationale: TPM's run directive at the bottom of .tmux.conf initializes all plugins. If the TPM directory does not exist, the run command silently fails and no plugins load.

rule.statement: Use tmux-resurrect and tmux-continuum for automatic session persistence. Enable @resurrect-capture-pane-contents so the text visible in each pane is saved and restored.
rule.rationale: Without pane contents capture, a restored session shows empty panes. With it, the last visible output (logs, process output) is replayed into each pane. Continuum auto-saves every 15 minutes and auto-restores on tmux server start.

rule.statement: Optional: add tmux-yank for system clipboard integration over SSH, but verify xclip or xsel is installed on the remote host first.
rule.rationale: tmux-yank copies to system clipboard (macOS pbcopy, Linux xclip/xsel). Over SSH, this requires xclip or xsel on the remote host.

rule.statement: Always use has-session before creating a named session; re-running a session-creation script should attach to the existing session, not fail or create duplicates.
rule.rationale: Session scripts are often called from aliases, shell startup, or agents. If the session already exists, recreating it destroys ongoing work.

rule.statement: Use different prefix keys for local and remote tmux to avoid double-prefix confusion: Ctrl+A locally, Ctrl+B remotely (or vice versa).
rule.rationale: When inside a local tmux session and SSH'd into a remote tmux, every prefix command requires sending the prefix twice (once for local tmux to ignore, once for the remote). Different prefixes eliminate this ambiguity.

rule.statement: Control background processes from scripts or agents via tmux send-keys to send commands and capture-pane to inspect pane output programmatically.
rule.rationale: send-keys allows scripts to interact with tmux panes without requiring an interactive terminal. capture-pane enables condition-based automation (check pane output, take action).
